content:
First observe that “adjacent‐cells must have opposite parity’’ forces the 3×3 grid to be viewed as a chessboard:

–  There are 5 black squares (say at (1,1),(1,3),(2,2),(3,1),(3,3)) and 4 white squares.  
–  The only way to alternate parity is to put all 5 odd numbers (1,3,5,7,9) on the black squares, and all 4 evens (2,4,6,8) on the white squares.

Hence without any symmetry identification there are
   5! ⋅ 4! = 120 ⋅ 24 = 2880
valid fillings.

We now mod out by the 4 rotations of the square.  By Burnside’s Lemma the number of orbits is
   (1/4) · [Fix(id) + Fix(90°) + Fix(180°) + Fix(270°)].

–  Fix(id) = 2880.  
–  A nontrivial rotation (90°,180°, or 270°) pairs or 4‐cycles distinct squares of the same color, forcing those squares to carry the same number in any “fixed’’ filling.  But our numbers 1,…,9 are all distinct, so no such nontrivial rotation can fix any filling.  Thus Fix(90°)=Fix(180°)=Fix(270°)=0.

Therefore the number of inequivalent (up to rotation) Isthmian arrangements is

   (1/4)·(2880+0+0+0)=720.

Answer: \(\boxed{720}\).
